title: Canard à l'orange
subtitle: Whole roast duck with a bitter orange and brandy sauce
excerpt: A whole duck roasted until the skin is lacquered and crisp, then served with a sauce bigarade built from the pan juices, orange zest, orange juice, and brandy. The sauce is sharp, glossy, and slightly bitter, cutting the richness of the duck. It is one of the great classic French dishes.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Canard à l'orange method and sauce bigarade composition using bitter orange (Seville orange) and roasting jus. Henri-Paul Pellaprat, L'Art culinaire moderne (1935), public domain via archive.org. Duck roasting temperatures and orange sauce proportions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce can be made a day ahead up to the point of adding the orange juice. Refrigerate. Finish with the orange juice and butter when the duck comes out of the oven.

glossaryTerms.definition: A classic French sauce made from bitter (Seville) orange juice and zest, combined with roasting juices and often a little brandy or Grand Marnier. Named for the bigarade, the bitter orange. It is the canonical sauce for duck à l'orange.
To add liquid (wine, stock, or spirits) to a hot roasting pan or saucepan after cooking, loosening the caramelised residue from the base. Those residues dissolve into the liquid and enrich the sauce.
To leave cooked meat uncovered for several minutes before carving. Resting allows the internal juices to redistribute through the flesh rather than running out when the meat is cut.

content.text: Canard à l'orange is a whole roast duck served with a sharp, orange-scented sauce built from the roasting juices. The duck gives you crisp skin and rich meat; the sauce gives you contrast, bright and slightly bitter against the fat. It is one of the great dishes of the French table.
The sauce matters more than anything else here. A good
sauce bigarade

is built from the roasting juices, orange zest, orange juice, and a dash of brandy. Seville oranges (bitter oranges) are the classical choice and are worth using in late January and February when they appear in shops. Out of season, a regular orange with a squeeze of lemon juice is a workable substitute.
Duck has a thick layer of fat under the skin. Score it before roasting so the fat renders out rather than sitting in a pool under the bird. The skin should come out lacquered and crackling.
What you need
You need a roasting tin large enough to hold the duck with a little space around it, and a small saucepan for the sauce.
Method
Score and season the duck

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional. Remove any giblets from the {{duck-whole}} duck. Score the skin all over in a crosshatch pattern, cutting through the fat but not into the flesh. Season generously with salt and pepper. Tuck the {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay inside the cavity along with a halved orange. Place the duck breast-side up in the roasting tin.
Roast the duck

Roast for 20 minutes at 200°C to start rendering the fat and crisping the skin. Reduce the oven to 180°C. Continue roasting for 60 to 70 minutes, pouring off the fat into a bowl every 25 minutes. The duck is done when the juices run clear from the thigh when pierced. Let the duck
rest
on a warm plate for 15 minutes, loosely covered.
Build the sauce bigarade

While the duck rests, pour off all but 1 tablespoon of fat from the roasting tin. Place the tin over medium heat on the hob. Add the {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and the {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar and let it bubble for 1 minute until lightly caramelised.
Deglaze

with the {{brandy}} of brandy if using and let it reduce by half. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and the {{orange-juice}} of orange juice. Stir in the orange zest. Simmer for 5 minutes until the sauce is slightly reduced and glossy. Off the heat, whisk in the {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter in pieces to finish the sauce. Taste and season.

items.symptom: Duck skin is flabby, not crisp
items.cause: Fat not rendered out properly; possibly oven not hot enough at the start
items.fix: Start the roasting at the higher temperature; pour off the rendered fat every 25 minutes so the skin is not sitting in its own fat

items.cause: Too much caramel or the orange is too sweet
items.fix: Add a little more red wine vinegar to balance; the sauce should be sharp-edged, not sweet

items.symptom: Sauce has broken or looks greasy
items.cause: Butter added to a sauce that was boiling, or added too quickly
items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat before whisking in the butter; add a few pieces at a time rather than all at once

items.symptom: Duck meat is still pink near the bone
items.cause: Undercooked; duck can look done from the outside while still raw inside
items.fix: Pierce the thigh at the thickest point; the juices must run clear, not pink. Return to the oven for another 15 minutes and check again

items.cause: Too much orange zest or the pith was included
items.fix: Use only the coloured zest, not the white pith. Soften the bitterness with a pinch more sugar, or strain the sauce and start the zest step again with a lighter hand

content.text: Where this dish lives

Duck with orange sauce is one of the most copied and most misunderstood dishes in the classical French repertoire. The original Escoffier version uses bigarade, the bitter orange (also called Seville orange), whose sharper juice and more fragrant zest make a sauce that is nothing like the sweet orange glaze that appeared on restaurant menus throughout the 1970s. The dish is at its best in winter, when Seville oranges are in season and the duck is well-fatted. Serve with gratin dauphinois or simple roast potatoes and a bunch of watercress.
